summary: The cricoid cartilage is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the respiratory tract.
The thyroid cartilage is the only complete ring in the respiratory tract.
The tracheal rings are complete rings that encircle the entire trachea.
The posterior cricoarytenoid is the only muscle that abducts the vocal cords (opens the glottis).
The lateral cricoarytenoid is the only muscle that abducts the vocal cords.
The thyroarytenoid muscle is the only abductor of the vocal cords.
The recurrent laryngeal nerve supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid, which is supplied by the external laryngeal nerve.
The external laryngeal nerve supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid.
The internal laryngeal nerve supplies all motor innervation to the larynx.
The cricothyroid membrane is the site for emergency cricothyrotomy.
The thyrohyoid membrane is the site for emergency cricothyrotomy.
The quadrangular membrane is the site for emergency airway access.
Glottic carcinoma presents early with hoarseness because even small tumours affect vocal cord vibration.
Glottic carcinoma presents late because the glottis has rich lymphatic drainage.
Supraglottic carcinoma presents earlier than glottic carcinoma with voice changes.
The narrowest part of the adult larynx is the rima glottidis, while in children it is the subglottis at the cricoid ring.
The narrowest part of the adult larynx is the subglottis, while in children it is the rima glottidis.
The narrowest part of the larynx is the same in adults and children.
The internal laryngeal nerve and superior laryngeal vessels pierce the thyrohyoid membrane to enter the larynx.
The internal laryngeal nerve pierces the cricothyroid membrane to enter the larynx.
The recurrent laryngeal nerve enters the larynx through the thyrohyoid membrane.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
